aliases: Vietnamese rice porridge with snakehead fish

english: Vietnamese rice porridge with snakehead fish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese snakehead fish porridge; mapped to the broader 'porridge food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Vietnamese snakehead fish porridge; A00GJ (fish soup/fish porridge) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Cháo cá lóc weighs approximately 450 g and represents a typical single-serve portion as sold at Vietnamese food stalls and restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, snakehead, cooked (closest available) combined with rice porridge (congee) composition data
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Vietnamese snakehead fish porridge as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked snakehead fish and plain rice congee/porridge, weighted to reflect the typical composition of this dish (approximately 30% fish, 65% rice porridge broth, 5% aromatics/herbs). The input record's per-100g values (energy 84.44 kcal, protein 6.67 g, fat 2.22 g, carbohydrate 8.89 g) are consistent with this composition and have been retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a specific entry for Cháo cá lóc.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese snakehead fish porridge (Cháo cá lóc). GI estimated at 78 (high) based on the closest analogous food: rice congee/porridge (GI ~78–81, University of Sydney GI Database). The high GI reflects the gelatinised starch structure of overcooked rice in porridge, which is rapidly digested. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 40.0) / 100 = 31.2, rounded to 31.5 accounting for the 450 g serving (8.89 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4.5 = 40.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg per 100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce when ordering Cháo cá lóc, limit portion to 200–250 g, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and choose fresh shallots over fried shallots to minimise additional sodium and fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~78) from gelatinised rice starch; a 450 g serving provides approximately 40 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate, which can cause significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g per meal, pair the porridge with additional lean protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid adding sugar or sweet condiments; the high water content of porridge reduces glycaemic density compared to steamed rice but does not eliminate the glycaemic concern.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at 0.56 g per 100 g and trans fat is 0 g; snakehead fish provides lean protein with some omega-3 fatty acids; however, sodium at 266.67 mg per 100 g warrants attention.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cháo cá lóc is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat and lean fish protein content; however, sodium intake should be managed by requesting reduced fish sauce and limiting portion size to 200–300 g to keep total sodium below 800 mg per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger (gingerols) provides evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; snakehead fish contributes lean protein and modest omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat is very low at 0.56 g per 100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cháo cá lóc is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request reduced fish sauce to keep sodium below 400 mg per 100 g, choose fresh shallots over fried shallots to avoid advanced glycation end-products, and consider supplementing with a side of fatty fish for stronger omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cháo cá lóc is a traditional Vietnamese rice porridge made with snakehead fish (cá lóc, Channa striata), a freshwater fish prized in Vietnamese and Southeast Asian cuisine for its firm, white, mildly sweet flesh. The dish is prepared by simmering white rice in water or light fish broth until the grains break down into a smooth, thick congee-like consistency, then adding thinly sliced or flaked snakehead fish that cooks gently in the hot porridge. It is typically seasoned with fish sauce and ginger, and served garnished with chopped spring onions, fresh herbs such as coriander and dill, and crispy fried shallots. The texture is soft, warming, and comforting, with a delicate savoury flavour from the fish and aromatic notes from the ginger and herbs. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 84 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 2.2 g fat (0.56 g saturated), and 8.9 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 267 mg. The high glycemic index (~78) from gelatinised rice starch means portion control is important for individuals managing blood glucose. The lean fish protein and anti-inflammatory ginger make it a suitable option for general wellness and rheumatoid arthritis management. Sodium content is borderline for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, and reduced fish sauce is recommended. The dish is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or light meal in Vietnam and is also found in Vietnamese restaurants and food stalls across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) carries a specific entry for Cháo cá lóc as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked snakehead fish and plain rice congee, weighted by typical recipe composition. The input record values are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (rice congee, GI ~78) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Vietnamese snakehead fish porridge as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish is not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~180 mg per 100 g based on fish and herb content but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. 3. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this traditional Vietnamese composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): snakehead fish and rice porridge contain no industrially produced trans fats; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for the component ingredients. 5. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on rice congee/porridge from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Cháo cá lóc was found in peer-reviewed literature. 6. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam as the dish's culinary origin, while noting it is commonly served in Singapore. 7. Aliases consistency check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present in flat aliases array.
